title: CPU Frequency Scaling Events
spl:
index=os sourcetype=linux_audit path="/sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu*/cpufreq/*" action=modified
| stats count by host, path
| where count > 10
description: Frequency scaling changes indicate thermal throttling or power management adjustments affecting workload performance.
value: Frequency scaling changes indicate thermal throttling or power management adjustments affecting workload performance.
implementation: Monitor /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu*/cpufreq/scaling_cur_freq for rapid changes. Create alerts when frequency scaling events occur frequently, indicating thermal or power issues.
knownFalsePositives: Governor and P-state changes are normal on idle and power-capped systems. Escalate sustained misconfiguration or unexpected limits, not every transition.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_nix, custom scripted input`.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=linux_audit OR custom:cpufreq`.
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Monitor /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu*/cpufreq/scaling_cur_freq for rapid changes. Create alerts when frequency scaling events occur frequently, indicating thermal or power issues.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=os sourcetype=linux_audit path="/sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu*/cpufreq/*" action=modified
| stats count by host, path
| where count > 10
```

Understanding this SPL

**CPU Frequency Scaling Events** ‚Äî Frequency scaling changes indicate thermal throttling or power management adjustments affecting workload performance.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=linux_audit OR custom:cpufreq`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_nix, custom scripted input`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: os; **sourcetype**: linux_audit. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=os, sourcetype=linux_audit. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, path** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where count > 10` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
On the host, compare with `top`, `htop`, `vmstat`, `iostat`, or `sar` as appropriate to this use case. For log-only detections, compare with the relevant file under `/var/log` (or `journalctl`) on a test host. Confirm that indexed event counts and field values line up with what you see on the system and that your role can search the right indexes and fields.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Timeline
grandmaExplanation: Frequency scaling changes indicate thermal throttling or power management adjustments affecting workload performance ‚Äî so you find out before users do when something is slowing down or breaking.
